original: St Martin I begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Duke Toto of Nepi stages a coup d'état in Rome and names his brother Constantine, Pope Constantine II. Today considered an antipope.
Austrian town of Bad Kleinkirchheim is first mentioned, in an ecclesiastical document
Pietro del Murrone elected as Pope Celestine V
Scotland and France form an alliance, the beginnings of the Auld Alliance, against England
German emperor Sigismund signs peace with Hussieten
Pope Nicolas V names Walram of Moers Bishop of the rich German city of Münster (voided 1452 during the conflict to control the city)
English fleet under Robert Devereux, 2nd Earl of Essex, captures Cadiz
John Guy sets sail from Bristol with 39 other colonists for Newfoundland
Battle at Lenz: Rebel Austrian Boers defeated
English settlers establish town of St Michael (later Bridgetown) on Barbados
1st recorded tornado in US (Essex County, Massachusetts)
Slave revolt on Curacao
First day of three day naval Battle of Chesma between the Russian and Ottoman Empires off the coast of Anatolia won by the Russians
The convention of Artlenburg leads to the French occupation of Hanover (which had been ruled by the British king)
Second Battle of Buenos Aires lost by invading British forces
War of 1812: Three weeks of British raids on Fort Schlosser, Black Rock and Plattsburgh, New York begin
Americans defeat British and Canadians at Chippewa, Ontario
France invades Algeria, begins a 40 year conquest
Provisional government forms in Oregon Country
Thomas Cook opens first travel agency
Committee of 9 appointed to establish civil government in Oregon Country
American sealing ship Captain N. C. Brooks discovers the uninhabited Midway Islands in the North Pacific Ocean and claims for the US
Battle of Carthage, Missouri - US General Sigel attacks pro-secessionist
Battle of Jackson, Mississippi and Battle of Birdsong Ferry, Mississippi
Battle at Chattahoochee River, Georgia (Skirmish at Pace's Ferry) fought during the Atlanta Campaign of the American Civil War
Battle of Smith's Expedition, Mississippi (Tupelo, Harrisburg)
United Kingdom creates the world's first speed limit law
US Secret Service begins operating under the Treasury Department
Trial against Kiowa chief Satanta (White Bear) and Big Tree, begins
The coat of arms of the Baku governorate was established
German consul-general Gustav Nachtigal takes possession of Cameroon
US Congress accept 2nd Chinese Exclusion Act
Columbia University 4's crew becomes first American boat to win at London's Henley Regatta (Visitors Challenge Cup)
Hail kills 16 horses in Rapid City, South Dakota

Lizzie Arlington becomes first woman to play professional men's baseball when she pitches 9th inning for the Reading Coal Heavers against the Allentown Peanuts; allows 2 hits and walks a batter but preserves 5-0 win
Compulsory education law passes Dutch 1st Chamber
All-rounder Monty Noble takes 6 for 52 as Australia wins the one and only cricket Test played at Sheffield's Bramall Lane, England
NY Giants 18-game winning streak ends as Phillies win 6-5 in 10 innings at Huntington Park
Niazi Bey, a chief organizer of the revolutionary movement in Turkey, raises the standard of revolt at Resna, Macedonia
Germany offers Austria war aid to fight against Russia in Serbia
1st general election in Netherlands
Uprising of social righteousness in Rio de Janeiro
Women 1st vote in Dutch elections, Christian parties win
Military revolt in Sao Paulo, Brazil
WOWO-AM, Indiana returns to air, 1 day after transmitter burns down
Austria signs a concordat (pact) with the Vatican, guaranteeing the rights and privileges of the Catholic Church in Austria
German party Catholic Center disbands
"Bloody Thursday" - Police open fire on striking longshoremen in San Francisco
1st "Hawaii Calls" radio program is broadcast
Tony Cuccinello (Brooklyn Dodgers) and Al Cuccinello (New York Giants) become first brothers to both hit a MLB homer in the same game; Dodgers win 14-4
120°F (49°C), Gannvalley, South Dakota (state record)
117°F (47°C), Medicine Lake, Montana (state record)
Republican offensive by Brunete in Spain
Spam, the luncheon meat is first introduced into the market by the Hormel Foods Corporation
Herb Caen's 1st column in San Francisco Chronicle
Flash floods kill 79 in Northeast Kentucky
Diplomatic relations broken between Britain and Vichy government in France
German occupiers disband political parties
Ian Fleminggraduates from a training school for spies in Canada
Battle of Gulf of Kula
Battle of Kursk, USSR: Operation Citadel: Nazi German Panzer Division assault begins what becomes the largest tank in history
Liberator bombers sink U-535 in Gulf of Biskaye
US invasion fleet (96 ships) sails to Sicily
Harry Crosby takes 1st rocket airplane, MX-324, for maiden flight
World War II: Liberation of the Philippines declared
Louis Réard's design for a bikini swimsuit debuts at a Paris fashion show
National Health Service established in the UK; Minister of Health Aneurin Bevan attends ceremony at Park Hospital in Davyhulme, Trafford
Law of Return passes, guarantees all Jews right to live in Israel
US forces enter combat in the Korean War for the first time, in the Battle of Osan
Phillies pitcher Robin Roberts hurls his 28th consecutive MLB complete game in a 2-0 win over Pittsburgh
WANC TV channel 21 in Asheville, NC (IND) begins broadcasting
B-52A bomber makes its maiden flight
The BBC broadcasts its first television news bulletin
Six week old British dock strike ends as London dockers return to work
France raises tobacco tax 20% due to war in Algeria
England cricket batsman Tom Graveney scores a brilliant 258 in the drawn 3rd Test v West Indies at Trent Bridge
First ascent of Gasherbrum I (Pakistani–Chinese border), 11th highest peak on earth
J Pengel forms government/Emanuels premier of Suriname
Indonesia restores constitution
Jack Gelber's "Connection" premieres in NYC
Mongolia adopts constitution
80 die in collisions in Algiers
KUSD TV channel 2 in Vermillion, SD (PBS) begins broadcasting
St Louis Cardinals first baseman Bill White smashes 3 HRs & a double in 9-1 rout of LA Dodgers
Algeria gains independence after 132 years of French rule

Massacre in Oran, Algeria: members of Algerian National Liberation Army kill Pied-Noir (Algerians of European descent) and European expatriates in ethnic genocide upon Algerian independence from France killing an estimated 95 to 400 people over 3 days
First Beatles' tune to hit US charts, Del Shannon's cover of "From Me to You" at no. 87
NASA launches Saturn IB rocket from Cape Canaveral, Florida
National Guard mobilizes in Omaha, Nebraska, after 3rd night of rioting
NYC Transit Authority raises basic bus and subway fare from 15 cents to 20 cents
Congo uprising under Belgian mercenary Jean Schramme
"Manifest of 1000 words" published in Prague
Rolling Stones play a free concert in London's Hyde Park
Air Canada DC-8 crashes 7 miles from Toronto's airport killing 109
26th amendment to the US constitution certified (reduces voting age to 18)
Simon Gray's "Butley" premieres in London
Pierre Messmer appointed French premier
Two Protestant brothers are found shot dead outside of Belfast (speculation that they were killed by Loyalists because they had Catholic girlfriends)
Isle of Man begins issuing its own postage stamps
Test cricket debut of English umpire Harold "Dickie" Bird v NZ at Leeds (66 Tests, 69 ODI)
Cape Verde Islands gain independence after 500 years of Portuguese rule
Pakistani army, led by General Muhammad Zia-ul-Haq, seizes power
Soyuz 30 spacecraft touches down in Soviet Kazakhstan
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Premier Begin's Likud party wins Israeli elections
Rajan Mahadevan recites 31,811 digits of π (Pi) from memory
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Challenger flies to Kennedy Space Center via Ellington AFB, Texas
France invades Algeria
Woman gives birth to baby 84 days after brain death (Roanoke, Virginia)
Supreme Court weakens 70-year-old "exclusionary rule"-evidence seized with defective court warrants can now be used in criminal trials
Nicholas Mark Sanders (England) begins circumnavigation of globe, covering 13,035 road miles in 78 days, 3 hr, 30 min
Inaugural edition of Goodwill Games open in Moscow, USSR
Wist Indies cricket paceman Malcolm Marshall takes career best 7-22, beat England by an innings & 156 at Old Trafford
American jockey Ray Sibille wins his 3,000th career race, aboard Sporting Surf at Pleasanton, California
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
MLB owners approve Colorado Rockies and Florida Marlins as new NL franchises to start play in 1993
Sixto Durán Ballén elected president of Ecuador
Kurd guerrillas murder 32 villager in East Turkey
Richard Chelimo run world record 10 km (27:07.91)
"Cracked Rear View" debut album by Hootie & the Blowfish is released
The United States announces it will no longer accept unrestricted immigration from Haiti
Angels score 3 top 1st, Oakland A's respond with 13 bottom 1st, highest-scoring 1st inning by 2 teams in MLB history, A's win 16-8
NY Yankees beat Baltimore Orioles 1-0 to improve record to 61-20, equalling best 81-game start in MLB history
Australia and East Timor sign an agreement to share royalties from oil and natural gas production in the Timor Sea, which separates the two countries
French driver Sébastien Bourdais wins first-ever night road-course event in Champ Car history on Cleveland's Burke Lakefront Airport layout
First direct presidential election held in Indonesia, eventually won by Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono
Emergency United Nations Security Council meeting at the U.N in New York City because of North Korean missile tests that day
North Korea tests at least two short-range Nodong-2 missiles, one SCUD missile and one long-range Taepodong-2 missile
Ethnic rioting breaks out in Ürümqi, Xinjiang, People's Republic of China
"Somebody That I Used to Know" single released by Gotye ft. Kimbra (Grammy Award Best Pop Duo/Group Performance, Record of the Year 2013)
Harness racing trainer/driver Dave Palone sets a new North American record for wins when he drives Herculotte Hanover to the winner's circle for his 15,181st career victory
South Korea announce plans to begin "scientific' whaling of minke whales
The Shard, the tallest building in Europe, is opened in London, at 309.6 metres (1,016 ft)
15 people are killed by a bomb attack on a Shia mosque in Baghdad, Iraq
101 people reported shot, 15 killed in Chicago, Illinois over 4th July weekend
Arsenal FC breaks club transfer record, paying about €53m for Lyon striker Alexandre Lacazette
Followers of Indian spiritual leader Ashutosh Maharaj win a court case three years after his death to keep his body in a freezer in case he returns to life
Shootout between drug cartels in Las Varas, Northern Mexico kills 14
Volvo Cars announces that all new models by 2019 will be either hybrids or battery-powered, the first automaker to do so
At least 24 killed in explosions at pyrotechnics workshops in Tultepec, Mexico
English rapper Stormzy announces partnership with Penguin Books, an imprint called #Merky Books
Heatwave in southern Quebec, Canada, kills 33
Saleswomen in Kerala, India, win right to sit down and take toilet breaks in new amendment to law
US Environmental Protection Agency head Scott Pruitt resigns amid allegations of misconduct
Ancient Iraqi city of Babylon is declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Former governor of Rio de Janeiro, Sérgio Cabral says he paid a $2 million bribe to secure votes for his city to be granted 2016 Olympics
Second stronger earthquake in Southern California at 7.1 magnitude centered near Ridgecrest
Florida reports a record 11,458 daily COVID-19 cases
Mexico's COVID-19 death toll passes 30,000 as it becomes the world's 5th worst-affected country
One of world's oldest pieces of art, 51,000-year-old deer bone carved by Neanderthals, announced discovered in Harz Mountains, Germany
Amid Greater Sydney's fourth major flood in 18 months, the Australian government declares a natural disaster as 45,000 people threatened with evacuation orders
In possibly largest-ever Chinese security breach, hacker offers to sell Shanghai police a database with information on one billion Chinese
Mathematics Fields Medal (presented every four years to mathematicians under 40) awarded to James Maynard, June Huh, Maryna Viazovska and Hugo Duminil-Copin in Helsinki
Mike Grier becomes the first Black manager in the NHL - of the San Jose Sharks
A wave of shootings across American during Fourth of July celebrations leaves a dozen people dead and more than 60 injured
Hottest global temperature ever recorded -  for third consecutive day - as average global temperature tops 17.18 degrees Celsius, tying with July 4, 2023
Meta launches Threads - Instagram's new public conversations app - a day later it becomes the most rapidly downloaded app ever with 30 million downloads
Rachel Reeves becomes the UK's first female Chancellor of the Exchequer, appointed by Labour PM Kier Starmer
Reformist Masoud Pezeshkian elected President of Iran, defeating  his conservative rival Saeed Jalili in a run-off election
Three new swimming sites open to the public on the Seine River in Paris, France for the first time since being outlawed in 1923 due to pollution and navigation concerns
Isaac Newton's great work, "Principia," is published by the Royal Society in England, outlining his laws of motion and universal gravitation
Ottoman troops storm citadel of Acrocorinth in the Peloponnese, massacring a large part of the population and selling the rest into slavery. InspiresLord Byron's poem "The Siege of Corinth"
Second Continental Congress drafts the Olive Branch Petition to KingGeorge III
Fugitive slaveFrederick Douglassdelivers his 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' speech to the Ladies' Anti-Slavery Society in Rochester, condemning the celebration as a hypocritical sham
DutchQueen Wilhelminameets SDAP-leader Pieter Jelles
António de Oliveira Salazarbecomes the premier and dictator of Portugal (1932-1968), founding the authoritarian Estado Novo (New State) regime
Lou Gehrighits record 17th career grand slam in Yankees 8-3 win v Washington, passesBabe Ruth's total
FDRsigns US National Labor Relations Act, effective the next day
NY Yankees outfielderJoe DiMaggiohits 20th HR of the season but 1st career grand slam in 8-4 win vs Boston Red Sox
Labour Party underClement Attleewins British parliamentary elections (results not declared until 26 July)
Cleveland Indians rookieLarry Dobybecomes 1st black player in AL when he strikes out in 6-5 loss vs Chicago White Sox
Invention of the junction transistor at Bell labs, invented byWilliam Shockley,John BardeenandWalter Houser Brattain(Nobel Physics Prize 1956)

SingerElvis Presley's 1st professional recording session (with guitarist Scotty Moore & bass player Bill Black) takes place at Sam Phillips' Memphis Recording Service in Memphis, Tennessee. The trio record four songs including their historic cover of Arthur "Big Boy" Crudup's song "That's All Right".
Ben-Gurion's Israeli government resigns
Greek-American sopranoMaria Callasmakes her final opera stage appearance in the title role ofGiacomo Puccini's "Tosca" at Convent Garden, London, England
GeneralJuvénal Habyarimanabecomes president of Rwanda in a military coup d'état
At Knebworth Festival in England,Pink Floyddebut their album "Wish You Were Here" with pyrotechnics and an exploding plane that flies into the stage
Robert Mugabe's Zimbabwe African National Union - Patriotic Front (ZANU-PF) party wins re-election in Zimbabwe for a second term
"Seinfield," originally titled "TheSeinfeldChronicles," screens its pilot episode starringJerry Seinfeld, Jason Alexander,Julia Louis-Dreyfus, and Michael Richards on NBC
Barry Bondshomers in Pittsburgh's 6-4 loss vs SF Giants, joining fatherBobby Bondsas MLB father-son HR record holders (408)
British singerRod Stewarthits his head on a lighting fixture while on stage and knocks himself out in Pine Knob, Michigan
South African PresidentP. W. Bothavisits ANC leaderNelson Mandela
Icelandic musicianBjörkreleases her 1st solo album "Debut"
MLB Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonleads off both games of Oakland's doubleheader vs Cleveland with homers (first time since 1913)
Amazon.com is founded byJeff Bezosin Bellevue, Washington
Toronto Blue Jays pitcherRoger Clemenscollects his 3,000th career strikeout in 2-1 win vs Tampa Bay (11th to achieve milestone)
Wimbledon Men's Tennis:Roger FedererbeatsAndy Roddickof the US 5-7, 7-6, 7-6, 3-6, 16-14 for a record 15th Grand Slam singles title
Enrique Pena Nieto of the Institutional Revolutionary Party is elected President of Mexico after a recount following irregularities
Former Argentine president and dictatorJorge Rafael Videlais sentenced to 50 years imprisonment for the abduction of about 400 babies born to political prisoners
FIFA Women's World Cup Final: Carli Lloyd scores a hat-trick as US beats Japan 5-2, in Vancouver
FBI releases report statingHillary Clintonwas "extremely careless" handling classified emails but doesn't recommend prosecution
Prime MinisterBoris Johnsonsays he will scrap all COVID-19 restrictions in England from 19 July, to become most unrestricted country in Europe
Legendary rock guitaristCarlos Santanacollapses on stage from dehydration and heat exhaustion in Pine Knob, Michigan
Keir StarmersucceedsRishi Sunakas Prime Minister of the United Kingdom in a landside victory for the Labour Party
US PresidentJoe Bidengives an interview to quell fears about his stamina and cognitive abilities with ABC's George Stephanopoulos

Ozzy Osbourne's "Back to the Beginning" final concert performance includes a reunion with original Black Sabbath band members at Villa Park in Birmingham, England, and raise funds for children's hospitals and Parkinson's disease research; other acts in the day-long festival includeMetallica, Slayer, Guns N’ Roses, Gojira, and Anthrax

Siege of Ostend begins when a Spanish force under Archduke Albert VII of Austria, with 12,000 men and 50 siege guns, lays siege to a small garrison of fewer than 2,000 Dutch at the strategically important fortress of Ostend, Flanders
Venezuelan Declaration of Independence: Seven provinces declare independence from Spain
Nancy Reagancuts a red, white and blue ribbon to reopen Statue of Liberty after refurbishment
SARS is declared "contained" by the WHO after affecting 26 countries and resulting in 774 deaths
